plan: 1. Define the ThreatRule and ThreatFinding interfaces in `src/modules/threat-model/threat-schema.ts` — ThreatRule contains: id, title, category (identity-exposure | social-engineering | permission-abuse | shoulder-surfing), evaluate function, explanation string. ThreatFinding contains: ruleId, title, severity ('Low' | 'Medium' | 'High'), description, evidence (string[]), userImpact, category. Also define ThreatInput containing: snapshot (SignalSnapshot), permissions (PermissionCheckResult[]), entropy (EntropyResult), permissionDebt (PermissionDebtResult), inferences (InferenceStatement[]).

2. Create the rules engine in `src/modules/threat-model/threat-rules.ts` — a pure function `evaluateThreatRules(input: ThreatInput): ThreatFinding[]` that runs each rule against the input and collects non-null findings. Each rule is a small pure function returning ThreatFinding | null.

3. Implement identity-exposure rule: triggers when entropy score ≥ 70 (high uniqueness) OR when renderer + timezone + languages combination is highly distinguishing. Severity: High when entropy ≥ 85, Medium when ≥ 70. Evidence lists the top contributing signals from entropy breakdown. Description explains cross-site tracking risk.

4. Implement social-engineering rule: triggers when inferences reveal geographic region + language + device type, giving attackers a basis for targeted phishing. Severity: Medium when ≥ 3 inferences with medium+ confidence, Low when ≥ 2 inferences. Evidence lists the inference statements. Description explains how combined profile data enables pretexting.

5. Implement permission-abuse rule: triggers when permission debt score ≥ 40 OR camera/microphone/geolocation are granted. Severity: High when camera+microphone both granted, Medium when any single high-risk permission granted, Low when debt ≥ 40 but no critical permissions. Evidence lists granted high-risk permissions. Description explains how granted permissions can be exploited by compromised sites.

6. Implement shoulder-surfing rule: triggers when screen resolution is large (≥ 1920px), device pixel ratio is standard (≤ 1), and touch is not supported (desktop in office setting). Severity: Low always (physical proximity risk). Evidence lists screen size and touch support. Description explains that large visible screens in non-touch setups suggest shared/office environments where visual eavesdropping is possible.

7. Add explainability: each rule includes a static `explanation` field (string) documenting why the rule exists and what thresholds it uses. Export a `getRuleExplanations(): Record<string, string>` helper so the UI or tests can verify all rules are self-documenting.

8. Write unit tests in `tests/us-027/threat-rules.test.ts` — test each rule in isolation with factory helpers for ThreatInput. Cover: identity-exposure triggers at boundary (score 69 vs 70 vs 85), social-engineering with varying inference counts, permission-abuse with each critical permission combo, shoulder-surfing with desktop vs mobile signals. Verify severity labels are exactly 'Low' | 'Medium' | 'High'. Verify all findings have non-empty evidence arrays and explanations.
9. Run `npm run lint`, `npm run typecheck`, `npm run test` to validate all checks pass.
testing_recommendations: Unit test each rule function independently with factory-built ThreatInput objects covering boundary conditions (e.g., entropy score at 69, 70, 85)
Test evaluateThreatRules aggregation: verify it returns findings from all matching rules and empty array when no rules trigger
Test that all severity labels are strictly 'Low' | 'Medium' | 'High' — no other values
Test that every ThreatFinding has non-empty evidence array and non-empty description/userImpact
Test getRuleExplanations returns an explanation string for every registered rule
Test graceful handling of missing/unavailable signal values (e.g., renderer='unavailable', empty permissions array)
Run npm run lint, npm run typecheck, npm run test as final validation
risks: Threshold tuning (entropy ≥ 70, debt ≥ 40) is subjective — may need adjustment after real-world testing to avoid false positives or missed findings
Shoulder-surfing rule relies on screen/touch heuristics that may misclassify tablets or external monitors
Content wording must avoid fear language per CLAUDE.md — descriptions need careful review to stay factual and measured
Dependency on US-025 InferenceStatement shape — if inference schema changes, social-engineering rule input breaks
